criteria_legend.C1: Accuracy — no fabricated symbols, image tags, config keys, env vars, paths; facts grounded in bundle.
criteria_legend.C2: Citation — cites a bundle file path and/or a real public URL referenced in the bundle.
criteria_legend.C3: Scoping — correct hardware/family scoping (BlueField Arm vs host, per-DPU, end-to-end pipeline, paired contract).
criteria_legend.C4: Actionability — concrete next step / verb / verification surface; routes via TASKS.md / Command appendix.
criteria_legend.C5: Self-consistency — does not contradict the bundle's own text; defers version-bound details to the live public guide.

prompts.prompt: I want runtime security on a fleet of BlueField-3s protecting a production database tier. Should I deploy DOCA Argus or build my own on top of doca-apsh, and why?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Argus is the production default for a packaged runtime-security workflow on BlueField + host with findings flowing into an existing SIEM (Splunk / ELK / Sentinel / generic syslog) — it ships detection policy, finding emission, and SIEM forwarding as one operationally-ready unit. doca-apsh is the lower-level App Shield library a developer uses to BUILD custom DPU-side security tooling and is the right answer ONLY when the team is genuinely building a custom security product that needs to ship its own decision logic. Path-selection rule lives in CAPABILITIES.md ## Safety policy and is enforced in TASKS.md ## configure step 1. Argus is a service, not a library; the operator does not link libargus.so.

prompts.prompt: What four configuration axes do I have to commit to before starting the DOCA Argus container?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Per the four-axis configuration table in Argus CAPABILITIES.md ## Capabilities and modes: (1) Detection policy — which classes of anomaly Argus alerts on (suspicious activity / integrity violations / operational anomalies); (2) Forwarding destination — local logs (smoke only) or a SIEM such as Splunk / ELK / Sentinel / generic syslog; (3) Sampling / sensitivity — the false-positive vs false-negative trade-off, sized against the workload's CPU / latency budget; (4) Host coverage — which host targets the Argus deployment monitors (BlueField itself / attached host / both / documented per-process scoping). The agent must force the four-axis decision BEFORE starting the container; the public DOCA Argus Service Guide is the source for the exact valid values.

prompts.prompt: My Argus container has been green for 24 hours but no findings have arrived at our Splunk. Where do I start debugging?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Walk the five-layer error taxonomy in order and do not skip down. Layer 1 (container runtime) — confirm not restart-looping, image tag correct, config mount path correct. Layer 2 (detection policy) — container green + no findings = detection policy too lenient for the workload pattern; this is the layer the calibration period is built to tune. Layer 3 (forwarding destination) — findings present in Argus's local feed but SIEM channel empty = forwarder misconfigured / SIEM endpoint unreachable / auth mismatched. The agent must NOT skip from a symptom directly to a detection-policy change; check whether the local finding feed shows findings first to distinguish layer-2 (no findings emitted) from layer-3 (findings emitted but not forwarded). NEVER silently disable a detector class to quiet the channel — re-tune policy or sampling, or explicitly document and time-box a disable.

prompts.prompt: Can I run the DOCA Argus container on my x86 host next to my application, or does it need to live somewhere specific?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Argus is a BlueField-Arm-only DOCA service container: it runs on the BlueField Arm cores, NOT on the host. The container is the unit of deployment, pulled from NGC and started under the BlueField OS container runtime per the public DOCA Container Deployment Guide. The host's relationship to Argus is purely as the observation target (Argus reads across the DPU boundary into the host) and as the network destination of the forwarder traffic to the SIEM. There is no host-side Argus binary, and host-side DOCA install is irrelevant to Argus. Running two Argus containers on the same BlueField with conflicting policies is a configuration error, not a redundancy strategy.

prompts.prompt: I have a non-DPU x86 host that owns a ConnectX SmartNIC and I want centralized gRPC-based management. Where should dmsd run, and what auth mode is appropriate for a multi-tenant production env?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: DMS supports three deployment shapes (host non-DPU, BlueField Arm, Kubernetes pod). For a plain ConnectX managed from an x86 host, the right shape is 'Host (non-DPU)' — DMS runs on the x86 host that owns the device. For a multi-tenant production env, route to the public DMS guide's Security Best Practices subsection BEFORE committing to an auth mode; the four documented modes are localhost (development only — binds to localhost and is never safe to expose), PAM (system user accounts + allowed_users list), Credentials (per public guide), and mTLS (per public guide). For multi-tenant production, mTLS or Credentials are the right space — quote from the live public DMS guide; do not paraphrase. Authorization is layered orthogonally: every issuing user must be in the dmsgroup Unix group regardless of which auth mode is chosen.

prompts.prompt: How do I issue a gNMI Set on /interfaces/interface/config/mtu against a managed BlueField, and how do I verify the change landed?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: DMS uses YANG modeled paths; /interfaces/interface/config/mtu is the example path referenced in the bundle. The documented validation idiom is the Set / Get pattern across Configuration vs State branches per the OpenConfig convention: issue the gNMI Set against the Configuration path, then issue a gNMI Get on the corresponding State path to confirm the change landed. The public DMS guide is the authoritative source for the exact supported path inventory and for the worked Get/Set execution examples; quote paths from the live guide and refuse to invent paths. Capability snapshot (TASKS.md test step 4) records which paths the deployment supports as the as-deployed answer.
citations: skills/services/doca-dms/CAPABILITIES.md#capabilities-and-modes (Configuration model — YANG dictionary; gNMI client surface)

prompts.prompt: My gNMI Subscribe stream keeps failing. Is DMS the right tool for streaming telemetry?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: No — DMS does NOT support gNMI Subscribe streaming telemetry. This is documented in CAPABILITIES.md ## Capabilities and modes as a documented constraint, and again in the path/operation layer of the error taxonomy: an attempted Subscribe surfaces as a path/operation-layer rejection. Streaming-telemetry questions go to the DOCA Telemetry Service (DTS), not DMS — route through doca-public-knowledge-map ## DOCA services for the DTS entry. If the user wants continuous telemetry, the right artifact is DTS; DMS's outward surface is configuration (gNMI Get/Set) and operations (gNOI).
citations: skills/services/doca-dms/TASKS.md#configure (step 3, Deferred task verbs — Continuous telemetry streaming)

prompts.prompt: After a DOCA upgrade on my management endpoint, dmsd rejects requests that worked yesterday. Where should I look first?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: DMS is in beta and its surface (supported paths, flag set, gNOI operation list) can change between DOCA releases — the DMS-specific version overlay in CAPABILITIES.md ## Version compatibility says to verify the public DMS guide version matches the DOCA install version on the target. Walk the layered debug ladder in order: transport (UNAVAILABLE / DEADLINE_EXCEEDED), authentication / authorization (UNAUTHENTICATED / PERMISSION_DENIED — check dmsgroup membership and allowed_users for PAM or cert trust for mTLS), and path / operation (INVALID_ARGUMENT — re-read the supported-paths reference; a previously-supported path may have moved). If DMS is running as a container, the container tag is the runtime version anchor and may lag the host package version — route to doca-version TASKS.md ## debug layer 2.

prompts.prompt: We have an SMPTE ST 2110 Rivermax broadcast plant on BlueField-3 and need PTP-locked time. What are the four PTP configuration axes and which Firefly profile maps to SMPTE?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Per the four-axis table in CAPABILITIES.md ## Capabilities and modes, the four PTP configuration axes are: PTP role (master / slave / boundary clock / transparent clock), Profile (default IEEE 1588 / SMPTE 2059-2 / G.8275.1 / G.8275.2 / custom), Domain number, Network interface (typically the wire-side BlueField port with hardware PTP timestamping enabled, NOT the management port), with Transport (L2 multicast / L3 unicast / L3 multicast) as a fifth knob. For SMPTE ST 2110 broadcast, the profile is SMPTE 2059-2 — Firefly drives the PHC with that profile and the Rivermax-using workload reads the disciplined PHC via doca-rmax; the host clock follower (chrony with the PHC source, or ptp4l/phc2sys) wires the host OS clock to the PHC. The end-to-end discipline is mandatory — Firefly alone is not a finished deployment. CAVEAT: the SKILL.md frontmatter Run-12 inventory block claims valid PROFILE values are 'default / media / telco-l2 / custom', which contradicts the CAPABILITIES.md four-axis table (and the SKILL.md's own Audience and When-to-load sections). The agent should quote profile names from the live public Firefly Service Guide.
citations: skills/services/doca-firefly/CAPABILITIES.md#capabilities-and-modes (Four-axis PTP configuration; Pairing with time-sensitive consumers)
skills/services/doca-firefly/SKILL.md (Audience and When-to-load sections; Run-12 inventory block — note self-contradiction)

prompts.prompt: Firefly container says PTP is locked and the PHC offset is tight, but `chronyc tracking` on the host shows seconds of drift. What layer is the problem?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: This is the classic layer-3 (Host-side follower) symptom in CAPABILITIES.md ## Error taxonomy: PTP locks (Firefly says synced; PHC offset tight) but the host OS clock drifts. Firefly disciplines the BlueField PHC; it does NOT discipline the host clock directly. The fix is on the host: confirm the host's chrony with the PHC source (or ptp4l/phc2sys reading the PHC) is the active follower, and that a competing NTP / chrony source is not winning. Use `chronyc tracking` and `chronyc sources` to confirm the Reference ID is the PHC and the offset is tight. This layer is owned by upstream Linux PTP / chrony, not by Firefly — Firefly is correct.

prompts.prompt: PTP sync acquired but the offset and jitter are in the tens of microseconds, way past spec. What's going on?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: This is layer-4 (PTP-aware path) in CAPABILITIES.md ## Error taxonomy: sync acquired, jitter wildly past the profile's spec. The cause is a non-PTP-aware switch in the path silently adding variable latency that PTP cannot correct from the endpoint. The fix is on the network side: insert boundary clocks at the non-PTP-aware switch, or replace the switch with a PTP-aware one. Do NOT try to fix this from Firefly config — it is a network-path property, not a Firefly knob. The safety policy is explicit: don't paper over a non-PTP-aware path with a Firefly config tweak.

prompts.prompt: My host is using chrony with internet NTP servers today and it has been fine. Do I actually need Firefly?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Probably not. Per the path-selection rule in CAPABILITIES.md ## Safety policy, Firefly is the right answer ONLY when PTP-grade time precision is actually required (SMPTE ST 2110 on Rivermax, 5G UPF, financial trading, sub-microsecond distributed-database time) AND a PTP-aware path is actually available (PTP-aware switches in the path, or boundary clocks). Do NOT reach for Firefly when chrony / NTP already meets the workload's time-precision budget (which is typical for normal workloads), when no PTP-aware switching exists, or when software-side time precision is sufficient. The right answer in those cases is to keep the host's existing chrony / NTP setup and tell the user honestly 'Firefly is the wrong tool here' rather than deploy it speculatively.

prompts.prompt: My doca-flow pipeline is dropping packets I expect to match. I want to mirror traffic to Flow Inspector and see what the hardware is doing. What's the contract between the inspector and my pipeline?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: The load-bearing capability of Flow Inspector is the two-sided mirror-action contract per CAPABILITIES.md ## Capabilities and modes: (a) the USER'S doca-flow / doca-flow-ct pipeline must program a mirror action that duplicates matching packets and forwards the copy to Flow Inspector's documented ingest target — without this, the inspector sees nothing; (b) the Flow Inspector container consumes the mirrored copy and exposes it at the chosen inspection depth on the chosen output destination. The mirror action is a doca-flow action kind (owned by doca-flow's library skill), NOT something Flow Inspector programs on its own. Stage the mirror narrowly (one match, one representor) per the smoke-before-bulk rule; attach a per-entry counter to the mirror entry so counters-first debug can answer 'is the mirror firing' independently of inspector health.

prompts.prompt: I deployed Flow Inspector and the container is up, but no traffic appears in the CLI. Where do I start?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: This is the most common first-app failure, and the agent MUST check layer 2 (mirror-not-wired) BEFORE re-examining inspector config. Per the error taxonomy: ask the user to show the mirror action in their doca-flow / doca-flow-ct pipeline spec, then read the mirror-entry counter to confirm the mirror is FIRING. If the counter is zero, the symptom is in the pipeline, not the inspector — route to doca-flow ## modify to add or fix the mirror; the inspector cannot fix a missing mirror action. Do NOT re-deploy the inspector without first clearing this layer. The cross-check between the pipeline counter and the inspector record count disambiguates 'mirror not firing' (layer 2) from 'inspector not receiving' (layer 1 / 5).

prompts.prompt: I want to run Flow Inspector permanently in production as our long-term observability source. Is that supported?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: No — Flow Inspector is explicitly a debug-time tool, not steady-state. Every mirrored packet costs cycles on the device that the doca-flow pipeline would otherwise spend on production work; leaving the mirror wired in production silently degrades dataplane throughput. Per the safety policy: enable the mirror at the start of a debug session, disable it at the end. For continuous visibility, the documented answer is to route to the DOCA Telemetry Service (DTS) instead — do NOT leave Flow Inspector hot in production as a substitute. The agent's job at the end of a debug session is to remind the user to remove or narrow the mirror action; silently leaving it wired is the load-bearing production-regression failure for this skill.
citations: skills/services/doca-flow-inspector/CAPABILITIES.md#safety-policy (Mirror is a debug tool, not steady-state)

prompts.prompt: Per-packet metadata vs per-flow aggregate vs raw packet content sampling — which inspection depth do I pick, and what's the safety posture of each?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Three documented depths per CAPABILITIES.md ## Capabilities and modes, each answering a different debug question: (1) Per-packet metadata — record per mirrored packet with 5-tuple, ingress port, action taken; highest resolution and highest overhead; pick when 'is the hardware seeing this specific packet'; (2) Per-flow aggregate — counters / state rolled up per flow; fewer records, summary-shaped; pick when 'is a class of flows reaching the pipe in expected volume'; (3) Raw packet content sampling — sampled stream of the actual packet bytes per documented sampling cadence; highest overhead and the ONLY depth that touches actual payload, so it carries the strongest privacy posture (treat captures as confidential by default; do not paste into public forums without redaction; do not export to destinations not authorized for payload data — same posture as core dumps). Safe default: the LIGHTEST depth that still answers the question, not the highest. Quote per-depth field names from the live public Flow Inspector Service Guide.
citations: skills/services/doca-flow-inspector/CAPABILITIES.md#safety-policy (Raw packet content sampling exposes payload bytes)

prompts.prompt: We just upgraded the host kernel from 5.14.0-362 to 5.14.0-427 and now the OS Inspector container is still green but the processes feed went silent. What broke?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: This is the canonical layer-3 (symbol map + memory regions) failure per CAPABILITIES.md ## Error taxonomy. OS Inspector inherits from App Shield the hard prerequisite that the symbol-map and memory-regions files MUST match the EXACT running host kernel build. A kernel upgrade silently invalidates the map; the container keeps running, the scanner keeps iterating, and the feed quietly stops representing reality. Resolution: confirm the current host kernel build version; regenerate the symbol map and memory-regions files against THAT build per the public DOCA App Shield guidance; replace the container mounts; restart the container; update the symbol-map version anchor in the deployment runbook. Do NOT respond to a layer-3 symptom by changing the scan policy — the fix is on the map.
citations: skills/services/doca-os-inspector/TASKS.md#modify (step 5 — refresh on every host kernel change)

prompts.prompt: Should I deploy OS Inspector or doca-argus to get host kernel state from my BlueField-3 forwarded into Splunk? What's the difference?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: They are different packaged services on the same APSH-class observation surface. Per the path-selection rule in CAPABILITIES.md ## Safety policy: OS Inspector is the RAW-INTROSPECTION service — it ships the APSH enumeration surface (processes, threads, loaded libraries, VADs, system modules) wired into DTS, with NO detection logic; the consumer side owns detection / correlation / archival. doca-argus is the RUNTIME-SECURITY service — it ships detection policy + finding emission + SIEM forwarding (Splunk / ELK / Sentinel / generic syslog) as one unit, with a calibration period and a tuned detector set. Use OS Inspector when you want raw observations and own the downstream detection; use Argus when you want findings about suspicious activity with detection policy built in. For 'forward into Splunk', either works; the question is whether you want raw enumerations (OS Inspector → DTS → Splunk) or detection findings (Argus → forwarder → Splunk).

prompts.prompt: The default OS Inspector scan policy defaults `processes_envars_info`, `processes_handles_info`, and `privileges_info` OFF. Should I turn them on?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Only deliberately and with a documented justification. The project's default scan policy is a minimum-exposure stance — `processes_info`, `threads_info`, `libs_info`, `vads_info`, `system_modules_info` are ON; `privileges_info`, `processes_envars_info`, `processes_handles_info` are explicitly OFF because they carry the highest-sensitivity host data (process command-line / environment variables, open handles, security privileges). Per the minimum-exposure-by-default rule in CAPABILITIES.md ## Safety policy: turning any off-by-default struct ON must be a deliberate decision with a documented reason and a documented downstream consumer that actually needs the data, not a 'might as well' flip. Silent expansions widen the data the DTS pipeline (and every downstream consumer) is now carrying about the host; unwinding that exposure later is harder than not opening it. Record any flip in the deployment capability snapshot with the reason and the date.
citations: skills/services/doca-os-inspector/TASKS.md#use (step 3 — Hold the minimum-exposure stance over time)

prompts.prompt: What's the five-axis configuration I must commit to before starting the OS Inspector container?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Per the five-axis table in CAPABILITIES.md ## Capabilities and modes: (1) DMA device + host VUID — the BlueField IBdev (a mlx5_* form named by the operator's live BlueField inventory) and the host VUID identifying the host whose memory is introspected; (2) OS type — Linux or Windows, drives which APSH telemetry-event registration the scanner uses; (3) Host kernel symbol map + memory-regions file — host-OS-version-specific artifacts generated against the exact running host kernel build; silently invalidated by a host kernel upgrade; (4) Scan policy — which APSH structs the scanner enumerates each iteration, starting from the project's minimum-exposure defaults; (5) Scan interval (the `-t` argument the public guidance names) — the cadence at which the scanner re-walks every enabled APSH struct, sized against the workload's BlueField CPU / DMA / host PCIe budget. The agent must force the five-axis decision before container start; the public OS Inspector / App Shield Agent guidance is the source for exact valid values.

prompts.prompt: My MPI cluster's host nodes link against doca-urom and host-CPU is the bottleneck on collectives. Should I deploy the UROM Service, and what runs where?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Yes if (a) the host fleet uses doca-urom, (b) the workload's host-CPU cost of posting RDMA work is the bottleneck, and (c) the BlueField generation in use supports the intended UCX components / collectives — confirm via a cap-query at deploy time. Per the publisher / executor paired-contract model in CAPABILITIES.md ## Capabilities and modes: host side runs the doca-urom LIBRARY (publisher) integrated into the user's HPC / UCX / MPI stack to ENQUEUE remote memory operations; DPU side runs the DOCA UROM Service CONTAINER (executor) on BlueField Arm to EXECUTE them against the RDMA fabric. Both halves are mandatory; a service running with no host paired through doca-urom is operationally idle. The host-library and service container versions are a paired contract — must agree per the DOCA Compatibility Policy.

prompts.prompt: Host's `doca_ctx_start` succeeded but the first enqueue returns DOCA_ERROR_NOT_PERMITTED even though the user is in the standard DOCA group. What layer is this?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: This is layer-2 (Service-side authentication / authorization) per CAPABILITIES.md ## Error taxonomy. DOCA_ERROR_NOT_PERMITTED at the host masks two distinct causes: (a) host OS doca_dev access denied (host-side fix in doca-setup) and (b) service-side authorization rejecting this host endpoint (service-side fix here). Walk the service-side logs for an authorization-rejection line; presence names cause (b), absence points back to (a). Resolution if (b): the service's host-authorization list does not include this host endpoint, OR the auth-credential material the host presents does not match what the service is configured to accept — fix in the service config's authorization axis (one of the three configuration axes) per the public DOCA UROM Service Guide's authentication / authorization section. Authentication is per-host-endpoint, not per-stream.

prompts.prompt: Host's `doca_urom_cap_*` says all-reduce is supported, but at runtime enqueue returns DOCA_ERROR_NOT_SUPPORTED. Service config issue, substrate, or version pairing?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: This is the canonical layer-5 (Paired-version mismatch) symptom per CAPABILITIES.md ## Error taxonomy: cap-query says yes (the host's library + device axis answered) but runtime says no. The host-side doca-urom library and the DPU-side service container are at versions the DOCA Compatibility Policy does not support pairing — cap-query answered for the host's pair, but the running service is at a different version that does not actually execute that variant. Cross-check `pkg-config --modversion doca-urom` on the host AND the service container tag on the BlueField against the DOCA Compatibility Policy (https://docs.nvidia.com/doca/sdk/doca-compatibility-policy/index.html) and the four-way-match rule in doca-version. The fix is either upgrading the service container or downgrading the host library; do NOT paper over with a retry, and do NOT change the service's exposed surface to 'fix' a version-pair gap.

prompts.prompt: My host fleet just upgraded doca-urom. Do I have to upgrade the service container on every BlueField too?
prompts.bundle_answer_summary: Almost certainly yes — the host-side doca-urom library version and the DPU-side service container tag are a paired contract per CAPABILITIES.md ## Version compatibility, and must agree per the DOCA Compatibility Policy. A host upgrade without a matching service upgrade (or vice versa) does NOT fail loudly — it fails SUBTLY, often as DOCA_ERROR_NOT_SUPPORTED for an operation family that exists on one side but not the other, or as silent stalls on collectives that one side believes the pair supports. Capture `pkg-config --modversion doca-urom` on the upgraded hosts; look up which service container tag the DOCA Compatibility Policy lists as compatible with that host library version; deploy that tag on every BlueField in the fleet. Do NOT memorize a tag — quote from the live public DOCA UROM Service Guide whose version matches the host library.

inaccuracies_surfaced.kind: SKILL.md self-contradiction + SKILL.md ↔ CAPABILITIES.md drift on PROFILE values
inaccuracies_surfaced.location: skills/services/doca-firefly/SKILL.md (Run-12 inventory block) vs skills/services/doca-firefly/SKILL.md (Audience / When to load) vs skills/services/doca-firefly/CAPABILITIES.md (four-axis table)
inaccuracies_surfaced.evidence: The Run-12 inventory block at the top of Firefly's SKILL.md asserts: 'The valid PROFILE values are exactly default / media / telco-l2 / custom (per doca_firefly.yaml comments) — the agent must not invent additional values.' But the same SKILL.md's Audience section says 'profile (default / SMPTE 2059-2 / G.8275.1 / G.8275.2 / custom)' and the When-to-load section repeats the SMPTE 2059-2 / G.8275.x naming. CAPABILITIES.md ## Capabilities and modes (Four-axis PTP configuration row) and the safety policy also use 'default IEEE 1588 / SMPTE 2059-2 (broadcast) / G.8275.1 / G.8275.2 / custom'. An external tester without the doca/ monorepo would see two incompatible profile-name sets within the same SKILL.md.
inaccuracies_surfaced.impact: Agent that follows the Run-12 inventory will refuse a legitimate SMPTE 2059-2 profile request as invalid, or invent the wrong knob; agent that follows the Audience/CAPABILITIES set will reject 'media' / 'telco-l2' as not-documented. The bundle cannot self-reconcile.

inaccuracies_surfaced.evidence: The block opens with 'The shipped doca_firefly.yaml exposes **five** PTP-stack subsystems via environment variables' and then explicitly numbers SIX subsystems (1. PTP, 2. PTP Monitor, 3. PHC2SYS, 4. PPS, 5. SyncE, 6. Firefly Servo). 'Five' and 'six' cannot both be right.
inaccuracies_surfaced.impact: Confuses the agent on the subsystem inventory; a count-aware response will say 'five' and then list six, or vice versa.

inaccuracies_surfaced.kind: Frontmatter env-var coverage — SKILL.md Run-12 inventory block
inaccuracies_surfaced.evidence: The Run-12 inventory block lists subsystems and env-var families: PTP_STATE/PTP_INTERFACE/PTP_CONFIG_FILE; MONITOR_STATE/MONITOR_CONFIG_FILE/MONITOR_CLIENT_TYPE/MONITOR_CLIENT_PHC2SYS_INTERFACE/MONITOR_CLIENT_CONNECTION_TIMEOUT; PHC2SYS_STATE/PHC2SYS_ARGS/PHC2SYS_CONFIG_FILE; PPS_STATE/PPS_DEVICE (with extra states enable_while_running and do_nothing); SYNCE_STATE/SYNCE_INTERFACE/SYNCE_CONFIG_FILE; SERVO_STATE/SERVO_CONFIG_FILE. Coverage looks self-internally complete, BUT: (a) the canonical PTP profile is not exposed as a PROFILE env var per the block — it's documented as a separate 'PROFILE' knob but the SKILL frontmatter description still talks about 'four PTP configuration axes (role / profile / domain / interface)' without naming the env-var override CONF_<SUBSYSTEM>_<section>_<key> pattern as the canonical surface. (b) The CONF_PTP_global_priority1 / CONF_SYNCE_global_backend / CONF_MONITOR_global_telemetry_export examples are given, but CONF_PHC2SYS_*, CONF_PPS_*, CONF_SERVO_* override patterns are not exemplified, leaving the surface uneven. Without the doca/ monorepo a tester cannot confirm whether all six subsystems actually accept the CONF_ override pattern.
inaccuracies_surfaced.impact: Agent answering 'what env vars do I need' may quote an uneven set; a tester noticing one subsystem has env-var examples and another does not will flag uneven coverage.

inaccuracies_surfaced.kind: Catalog drift — README.md vs SKILL.md/CAPABILITIES.md scoping
inaccuracies_surfaced.evidence: README.md describes doca-flow-inspector as 'emits a stream for off-host analysis (containerized, kubelet-standalone).' But the service's own CAPABILITIES.md ## Capabilities and modes (Deployment shape) is explicit: 'The agent's rule: the inspector consumes traffic *locally* on the BlueField. There is no documented host-side deployment shape and no documented "inspect a remote BlueField's pipeline from a different host" pattern.' The README's 'off-host analysis' framing contradicts the bundle. Additionally 'kubelet-standalone' is not surfaced anywhere in the service's SKILL/CAPABILITIES/TASKS — the deployment shape is documented only as 'long-running container on BlueField Arm, per the Container Deployment Guide pattern'.
inaccuracies_surfaced.impact: Catalog reader gets a wrong product-family scoping (off-host); an agent that takes the README at face value will mis-route the user toward a host-side or remote-host deployment that the service doesn't support.

inaccuracies_surfaced.location: README.md line 234 vs skills/services/doca-urom-svc/SKILL.md frontmatter + CAPABILITIES.md
inaccuracies_surfaced.evidence: README.md describes doca-urom-svc as 'The deployed service half of UROM — manages offloaded RDMA flow state.' The service's SKILL.md and CAPABILITIES.md describe it as the DPU-side EXECUTOR of remote memory operations (puts, gets, atomics, collectives) for MPI/UCX HPC workloads — not as a 'flow state' manager. The 'manages offloaded RDMA flow state' framing reads like a doca-flow / RDMA-flow-cache offload, not a UROM service.
inaccuracies_surfaced.impact: An agent or operator reading the README catalog will mis-classify the UROM service as a network-flow-state controller rather than an HPC remote-memory executor; this is exactly the kind of catalog drift the tester is asked to surface.

inaccuracies_surfaced.evidence: TASKS.md says 'Layered diagnosis. Walk the layers in this order; do not skip down without clearing the layer above. The five layers match CAPABILITIES.md ## Error taxonomy.' But the verb then lists SEVEN numbered layers (1. Container runtime, 2. Service-side authorization, 3. Service-side resource, 4. Underlying RDMA substrate, 5. Paired-version mismatch, 6. Performance-not-helping, 7. Cross-cutting). The prose 'five' contradicts the listing of seven. CAPABILITIES.md error taxonomy actually has five layers, so TASKS.md mis-cites its own cross-reference.
inaccuracies_surfaced.impact: Agent walking the debug ladder may stop after layer 5, missing the performance-not-helping diagnosis (layer 6) which is explicitly called out as 'most often not a bug'.

inaccuracies_surfaced.kind: Catalog drift — README.md framing vs SKILL.md/CAPABILITIES.md
inaccuracies_surfaced.evidence: README.md describes doca-argus as 'Cybersecurity-monitoring service on BlueField — process / network / file telemetry stream.' The SKILL.md and CAPABILITIES.md describe Argus as a RUNTIME-SECURITY service whose primary output is FINDINGS (alerts) forwarded to a SIEM with detection policy, calibration period, and a four-axis configuration. 'Process / network / file telemetry stream' is closer to a raw introspection feed (which is what doca-os-inspector is) than to Argus.
inaccuracies_surfaced.impact: Mild — an agent could mis-classify Argus as a metrics/telemetry shape rather than a findings shape and then route to/away from the wrong companion (DTS / OS Inspector / Argus).

inaccuracies_surfaced.kind: AgentSkills.io-style verb-set deviation from the bundle norm
inaccuracies_surfaced.evidence: The other five services in scope use the standard 6-verb pattern 'configure → build → modify → run → test → debug'. doca-os-inspector adds two extra verbs (## install at the top and ## use at the end), giving 'install → configure → build → modify → run → test → debug → use'. The SKILL.md and TASKS.md are internally consistent on this, but the deviation from the bundle's shared service-skill shape (as called out in doca-argus / doca-firefly / doca-flow-inspector / doca-urom-svc Related-skills sections — 'the agent reading any two of these should see the same service-skill shape') is itself an inconsistency a tester would notice.
inaccuracies_surfaced.impact: Minor — does not break correctness, but it weakens the agent's pattern recognition across services and complicates a uniform 'service-skill' authoring contract.

inaccuracies_surfaced.kind: Aspirational claim against the bundle's own '## test' iterative-loop overlay row count
inaccuracies_surfaced.evidence: The overlay's last row says '1..5 → ## run | Each loop iteration ends with a smoke; if all five pass, hand off to live ## run traffic'. But ## test only documents 5 numbered steps and one of them is the negative authorization test (step 2) which is explicitly a destructive check that gets restored — that is not really a 'smoke that must pass'. Minor framing inconsistency.
inaccuracies_surfaced.impact: Very minor; agent following the loop will run all five steps regardless.

install_paths_observed.status: matches DOCA standard install location; not stale

agentskills_io_compliance_violations.issue: SKILL.md frontmatter description is followed by a long 'Subsystem inventory (Run-12 correction)' blockquote BEFORE the H1 title — the YAML frontmatter ends at line 29 (---), the H1 is at line 31. That's compliant. HOWEVER the Run-12 inventory block embedded between the frontmatter and the canonical sections introduces a parallel taxonomy (five-or-six subsystems, PROFILE values default/media/telco-l2/custom) that contradicts the canonical sections of the same SKILL.md and of the CAPABILITIES.md. AgentSkills.io expects SKILL.md to be the load-bearing single source of truth for the skill's claims; the bundle violates that internal-consistency expectation by carrying two incompatible authoritative claims in one SKILL.md.

Verb-set deviates from the bundle-norm 6-verb pattern (configure / build / modify / run / test / debug) by adding 'install' and 'use'. AgentSkills.io itself does not mandate a specific verb set, but the bundle's other service skills do and describe themselves as sharing the same shape, so this is bundle-internal noncompliance with its own self-stated convention.

wrong_product_family_scoping.claim: README.md catalog describes it as 'kubelet-standalone' with 'off-host analysis'
wrong_product_family_scoping.bundle_truth: CAPABILITIES.md states the inspector is BlueField-local with no documented host-side or remote-host deployment shape

wrong_product_family_scoping.claim: README.md catalog frames it as managing 'offloaded RDMA flow state'
wrong_product_family_scoping.bundle_truth: SKILL.md and CAPABILITIES.md frame it as the DPU-side EXECUTOR of HPC remote-memory operations and collectives (paired with the host doca-urom library)

CONFIG_FILE_env_vars_missing_or_not_appl: PPS has no _CONFIG_FILE — PPS uses PPS_DEVICE instead, which the block does name
subsystem_env_var_coverage_check_doca_fi.PROFILE_env_var_assertion: Block says 'valid PROFILE values are exactly default / media / telco-l2 / custom'
subsystem_env_var_coverage_check_doca_fi.PROFILE_value_conflict: Same SKILL.md's Audience and When-to-load sections list 'default IEEE 1588 / SMPTE 2059-2 / G.8275.1 / G.8275.2 / custom' as profile values; CAPABILITIES.md four-axis table agrees with Audience and contradicts the Run-12 block
subsystem_env_var_coverage_check_doca_fi.summary: PROFILE / SUBSYSTEM env vars are NOT correctly listed in a self-consistent way — STATE / CONFIG_FILE / INTERFACE / DEVICE env-var coverage per subsystem is internally consistent, but PROFILE values fundamentally disagree between the Run-12 block and the canonical Audience / When-to-load / CAPABILITIES sections, and CONF_<SUBSYSTEM>_<section>_<key> override-pattern examples are only given for 3 of the 6 subsystems.

summary_text: B4 (Wave-B, without doca/ monorepo) graded 24 prompts across 6 services.
Per-prompt grading C1..C5 (Accuracy, Citation, Scoping, Actionability, Self-consistency) — pass requires all 5.
All 24 prompts answered from the bundle PASS overall: 24/24 pass; per-criterion 24/24 PASS each.

Highest-value tester-visible defect: doca-firefly SKILL.md is self-contradictory on PROFILE values (Run-12 block: default/media/telco-l2/custom) vs SKILL.md's own Audience / When-to-load / CAPABILITIES.md (default IEEE 1588 / SMPTE 2059-2 / G.8275.1 / G.8275.2 / custom).
doca-firefly Run-12 inventory block also self-contradicts on subsystem count: declares 'FIVE PTP-stack subsystems' then numbers SIX (PTP, PTP Monitor, PHC2SYS, PPS, SyncE, Firefly Servo).
doca-firefly CONF_ override-pattern examples cover only 3 of the 6 subsystems (PTP, SYNCE, MONITOR); PHC2SYS, PPS, SERVO are not exemplified.

Catalog drift surfaced: README.md mis-scopes doca-flow-inspector as 'off-host analysis / kubelet-standalone' (CAPABILITIES.md says BlueField-local only) and mis-describes doca-urom-svc as 'manages offloaded RDMA flow state' (it is the HPC remote-memory-operation executor paired with the host doca-urom library).

doca-urom-svc TASKS.md ## debug intro claims 'five layers' but lists seven (drift between prose and enumeration); the seven include performance-not-helping and cross-cutting layers beyond the CAPABILITIES.md five.

doca-os-inspector TASKS.md uses an 8-verb pattern (install / configure / build / modify / run / test / debug / use); the bundle's other five services use the canonical 6-verb pattern — bundle-internal verb-set drift, minor.
No stale install paths found; /opt/mellanox/doca in doca-dms compatibility frontmatter matches the canonical DOCA install location.
